reasoning: The missing `return` before `wp_send_json_error($status)` at line 48 is a real code quality defect (compare line 38 which correctly uses `return wp_send_json_error(...)`). However, this does not constitute a security vulnerability for the following reasons: (1) Auth gate is solid: the handler is registered only under `wp_ajax_` (authenticated users only) and line 35 enforces both `install_plugins` capability and a valid nonce before any logic runs. An unauthenticated user or unprivileged user cannot reach line 45 at all. (2) The fallthrough path (lines 51-117) uses only a hardcoded class constant `self::CONTENT_EMBED_LINK` as the install source. There is zero attacker-controlled input reaching the plugin installer. The worst-case outcome of fallthrough is a redundant install attempt of the same HubSpot plugin from the same hardcoded URL. (3) In production WordPress 4.7+, `wp_send_json_error()` internally calls `wp_die()`, which terminates execution reliably. The fallthrough scenario only materialises in test/mocked environments where `wp_die()` is suppressed, which is not an attacker-accessible condition. (4) The finding itself characterises this as 'a defense-in-depth gap rather than an immediately exploitable vulnerability,' which is the correct framing. There is no attacker-controlled sink reachability, no privilege escalation, and no meaningful impact even in degraded environments. Classification: code quality / informational, not a security finding.
